full_response:
Step 1 :  Choose coordinates  

Let the Earth be at the origin of an inertial frame S.  
The spaceship moves with constant velocity  

  v = βc  êx ,  β = 1/2.  

Its trajectory is the straight line  

  x = vt ,  y = b ,  z = 0                                  (1)

so that at t = 0 the ship passes the point (x = 0 , y = b ); this is the instant of closest approach.

Step 2 :  Direction of the radio signal at the reception event  

The radio signal that the astronauts receive at t = 0 must have been emitted at an earlier time t_e.  
Because radio waves propagate at the speed of light, the emission and reception events are connected by a null interval,

  c(t_r – t_e) = | r_ship(t_r) – 0 | .

With t_r = 0 and c = 1 (relativistic units)

  | r_ship(0) | = | (0 , b , 0) | = b  ⇒  –t_e = b .          (2)

Thus the wave travels from the Earth straight up the y-axis; in the Earth frame its wave-vector is perpendicular to the ship’s velocity:

  k · v  ⇒  θ_S = 90°  ⇒  cos θ_S = 0 .                      (3)

Step 3 :  Relativistic Doppler formula  

For an electromagnetic wave of frequency f_S in the source (Earth) frame, the frequency seen by an observer moving with speed v that makes an angle θ_S (measured in the SOURCE frame) with the direction of propagation is

  f_O = γ f_S (1 – β cos θ_S) .                              (4)

This formula appears on the 8.033 sheet.

Step 4 :  Apply to the present situation  

Because cos θ_S = 0 in our geometry, eq. (4) simplifies to the purely transverse Doppler shift

  f_O = γ f_S .                                              (5)

The Lorentz factor for β = 1/2 is

  γ = 1 / √(1 – β²) = 1 / √(1 – 1/4) = 1 / ( √3 / 2 ) = 2/√3
   ≈ 1.1547 .                                               (6)

The broadcast frequency is f_S = 700 kHz, so the astronauts must tune to

  f_O = γ f_S = (2/√3) × 700 kHz
      ≈ 1.1547 × 700 kHz
      ≈ 8.08 × 10² kHz .                                    (7)

Numerically,

  f_O ≈ 808 kHz .                                            (8)

\boxed{\,f_{\text{astronauts}} \;\approx\; 8.1 \times 10^{2}\ \text{kHz}\;( \text{about } 808\ \text{kHz})\,}

Confidence: 63%
